## A. ĐÃ GIẢI (RESOLVED)

Tất cả resolution có `status=resolved`, đã phản ánh đúng các correction từ adversarial verification.

| Câu hỏi | Kết luận (đã hiệu chỉnh theo verification) | Nguồn |
|---|---|---|
| API external-backend / full-surface có expose **parsed escape-sequence / terminal-state stream** cho host không, hay chỉ rendered surface? Host đọc được OSC 133 / cursor / cell grid để làm client-side block UI? | **Surface embedding API (`ghostty.h`) hoàn toàn opaque**: KHÔNG có API stream escape sequence đã parse, KHÔNG expose cursor position / cell grid dạng struct. Host chỉ có 2 đường đọc nội dung: (a) `ghostty_surface_read_text` / `ghostty_surface_read_selection` — snapshot UTF-8 của 1 vùng viewport; (b) action callbacks (`GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED` mang exit_code + duration_ns, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PWD`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PROGRESS_REPORT`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_CELL_SIZE`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SCROLLBAR`). OSC 133 được xử lý nội bộ, host không thấy raw bytes. **CORRECTION quan trọng từ verification**: có 1 thư viện RIÊNG `libghostty-vt` (`include/ghostty/vt.h`) *có* expose cell grid (`ghostty_terminal_grid_ref()`) và cursor (`GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_CURSOR_X/Y/VISIBLE`) — NHƯNG đó là VT-emulator standalone, không có cầu nối với `ghostty_surface_t`; cả vvterm, Lakr233, daiimus đều chỉ dùng `ghostty.h`, vvterm còn chủ động loại trừ `vt.h` khỏi bridging header. → Muốn client-side block UI: dùng `read_text` + action callbacks để suy luận, KHÔNG có grid API trên surface. | daiimus `include/ghostty.h:1217-1232`, `:927-1000`; ghostty-org `src/termio/stream_handler.zig:333,1083-1093`, `src/Surface.zig:1132-1150`; Lakr233 `TerminalCallbackBridge.swift:28-151`, `InMemoryTerminalSession.swift:74-109`; vvterm `ghostty-bridging-header.h:13`; ghostty-org `include/ghostty/vt/terminal.h:645-700`, `vt/grid_ref.h` |
| Alt-screen switch (DECSET 1049 smcup/rmcup) có hoạt động đúng khi feed bytes qua external-backend + render bằng libghostty (fullscreen TUI như vim / Claude Code fullscreen)? | **Có, hoạt động đúng.** Cả 3 feed function đều dẫn về cùng 1 path: `ghostty_surface_write_buffer` (Lakr233), `ghostty_surface_write_output` (daiimus), `ghostty_surface_feed_data` (wiedymi) → `core_surface.io.processOutput(...)` → `terminal_stream.nextSlice(buf)` — cùng VT parser với PTY exec backend. Mode 1049 wired qua `switchScreenMode(.@"1049", enabled)`, full DECSET semantics (saveCursor, switch sang alternate, eraseDisplay, cursorCopy, restoreCursor khi rmcup). Backend chỉ là I/O source khác nhau cùng feed 1 byte stream vào cùng 1 parser. **Correction nhỏ (không ảnh hưởng kết luận)**: `saveCursor()` thực ra ở `Terminal.zig:3068` (trước switchScreen), không phải trong range 3097-3105 như claim gốc; range đó là bước cursorCopy. | daiimus `src/apprt/embedded.zig:1966-1973`, `src/termio/Termio.zig:764-824`, `stream_handler.zig:1093-1094`; ghostty-org `src/terminal/modes.zig:294`, `src/terminal/Terminal.zig:3046-3115`, `stream_handler.zig:707-708`; Lakr233 `0002-host-managed-io.patch:235-242` |
| Keyboard: libghostty surface biến key event thành bytes qua write-callback như thế nào — tự encode kitty keyboard protocol hay host phải encode? | **Ghostty tự encode TẤT CẢ** (đường chính). Host truyền `ghostty_input_key_s` (action, mods, consumed_mods, keycode, text, unshifted_codepoint, composing) → `ghostty_surface_key()` → `Surface.encodeKey()` → `input.key_encode.encode()`, đọc live terminal state (kitty_flags, cursor_key_application/DECCKM, modify_other_keys_state_2, alt_esc_prefix) để emit đúng bytes. Write-callback / receive_buffer nhận bytes ĐÃ encode. **⚠ CORRECTION lớn (claim gốc bị REFUTED ở phần cuối)**: khẳng định "UITerminalView+Keyboard.swift never encodes bytes manually" là SAI trong Lakr233/libghostty-spm. Với backend `.inMemory` và KHÔNG có modifier, `TerminalHardwareKeyRouter.directControlInputForUIKit()` **hardcode raw VT100 sequences** cho 1 tập phím navigation (Backspace→0x7F, Tab→0x09, Delete→`\x1B[3~`, Home/End, PageUp/Down, 4 arrow keys→`\x1B[A/B/C/D`) và gửi thẳng qua `session.sendInput()`, **bypass hoàn toàn `ghostty_surface_key`**. Các sequence này protocol-blind (không tôn trọng kitty mode / DECCKM). Bypass chỉ áp dụng khi: (1) backend inMemory, (2) không modifier, (3) không IME marked text — mọi phím khác và mọi navigation-có-modifier vẫn đi qua ghostty encoder. → **Khuyến nghị thực tế**: nếu muốn ghostty encode tất cả (đúng với remote PTY có thể đang ở kitty/DECCKM mode), KHÔNG dùng bypass path của Lakr233; route mọi key qua `ghostty_surface_key`. | daiimus `src/Surface.zig:3489-3589`, `src/input/key_encode.zig:14-89`, `src/termio/External.zig:1-56`, `include/ghostty.h:322-330,1180`; Lakr233 `UITerminalView+Keyboard.swift:66-180`, `TerminalHardwareKeyRouter.swift:35-88`, `InMemoryTerminalSession.swift:156-202`, `TerminalSurface.swift:32-43` |
| Thread-safety: phải gọi `ghostty_surface_feed_data` / `write_buffer` từ thread/queue nào? | **Thread-safe nội bộ, nhưng phải serialize per-surface.** `write_buffer`/`write_output` gọi `processOutput()` vốn acquire `renderer_state.mutex` trước khi chạm terminal state. daiimus doc hóa rõ: "NOT safe to call concurrently on the SAME surface — caller must serialize; IS safe concurrently on DIFFERENT surfaces." → Gọi từ thread I/O riêng (không cần main thread) là đúng và đúng ý đồ; chỉ cần serial dispatch cho mỗi surface. `@MainActor` ở VVTerm.feedData() là convention của VVTerm, KHÔNG phải yêu cầu của libghostty. | ghostty-org `src/termio/Termio.zig:643-648`; daiimus `src/apprt/embedded.zig:1961-1965`; vvterm `Ghostty.Surface.swift:213`; Lakr233 `InMemoryTerminalSession.swift:126-148` |
| `InMemoryTerminalSession` của Lakr233 cung cấp chính xác cái gì, và carry external-backend patch hay plain upstream? | **Là Swift wrapper trên host-managed I/O backend, KHÔNG phải plain upstream.** Yêu cầu patch `0002-host-managed-io.patch` (thêm `GHOSTTY_SURFACE_IO_BACKEND_HOST_MANAGED`, `ghostty_surface_write_buffer`, `ghostty_surface_process_exit`, `HostManaged.zig`). Cung cấp: (1) `receive(Data/String)` → feed bytes vào parser qua `write_buffer`; (2) write callback (`receiveBufferCallback` → `writeHandler`) khi cần gửi user input ra host; (3) resize callback; (4) `finish(exitCode:runtimeMilliseconds:)` → `process_exit`; (5) `readViewportText()` qua `read_text`. Input keyboard tách riêng: host gọi `surface.sendKeyEvent(...)`, ghostty encode rồi fire write callback. **KHÔNG expose parsed VT state stream** (cùng giới hạn Q1). | Lakr233 `InMemoryTerminalSession.swift:1-254`, `TerminalController+Surface.swift:53-66`, `0002-host-managed-io.patch`, `Patches/ghostty/README.md`, `AGENTS.md:74-75` |
| TCP byte-stream: in-order lossless delivery có nghĩa chỉ cần buffering/reassembly đơn giản (accumulate tới full frame), KHÔNG cần loss recovery? | **Đúng.** TCP đảm bảo mọi byte tới đúng 1 lần, đúng thứ tự (retransmit nằm trong tầng TCP, invisible với app). Một `recv()` có thể trả ít hơn số byte 1 lần `write()` → 1 escape sequence có thể bị tách qua 2 read. Cơ chế DUY NHẤT cần: receive buffer / VT parser stateful giữ state qua các read (Ghostty `stream.zig:503-515` comment xác nhận "last chunk can put us mid control sequence"). KHÔNG cần sequence numbers, ACK, dedup, reorder buffer. Giống hệt mọi local PTY reader (SwiftTerm `LocalProcess.swift:64` đọc 128KB/callback không có loss logic). | RFC 9293 §1.1; RFC 793; SwiftTerm `LocalProcess.swift:64`; ghostty `stream.zig:503-515`, `Exec.zig:1302-1338`, `Termio.zig:643-693` |
| `openpty()` + `posix_spawn(POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID, login_tty)` vs `forkpty()` cho host PTY bridge trên macOS — pattern an toàn nào? | **`openpty()` + `posix_spawn`/swift-subprocess với `createSession=true` là pattern an toàn.** `forkpty()` KHÔNG an toàn từ Swift: fork-without-exec để runtime Swift (phụ thuộc malloc mutex) ở trạng thái bất định trong child (Apple DTS thread 130254 xác nhận verbatim). Pattern đúng: openpty → swift-subprocess `createSession:true` (nội bộ fork()+setsid()+posix_spawn(POSIX_SPAWN_SETEXEC)) → dup2 slave fd vào stdin/stdout/stderr → close slave ở parent. **Correction**: trong SwiftTerm đã ship, path swift-subprocess đang bị `#if false` (compiled out); active path THỰC TẾ dùng `forkpty()` qua `PseudoTerminalHelpers.fork()` — đây là **Swift gọi libc forkpty** (không phải "pure-C wrapper" như claim gốc), an toàn vì child gọi `execve()` ngay, không chạy Swift runtime code. Kết luận cho daemon Swift mới: dùng openpty + swift-subprocess createSession:true. | Apple DTS thread 130254; SwiftTerm `LocalProcess.swift:61`, `Pty.swift:97`; swift-subprocess `process_shims.c:102-241`, `Subprocess+Darwin.swift:61` |
| OSC 133 shell integration có hoạt động qua setup này (host chạy login shell trong PTY, byte stream qua TCP tới client)? Có gì network-specific cản không? | **Hoạt động không cần sửa gì.** OSC 133 là pure byte-stream annotation: shell hook (zsh precmd/preexec, bash PROMPT_COMMAND/DEBUG trap) ghi thẳng escape sequence ra TTY fd (`print -nu $_ghostty_fd '\e]133;C\a'`), chảy qua PTY master → TCP → client parser. ZERO network logic, không cần daemon/agent/extension. VT parser byte-source-agnostic. **Correction nhỏ**: Warp's block dùng DCS-based JSON protocol RIÊNG (KHÔNG phải OSC 133) — viện Warp làm bằng chứng "OSC 133 block semantics work" là category error; bằng chứng đúng là chính spec OSC 133 (transport-agnostic) + shell integration source (ghi thẳng TTY, không qua daemon). | Per Bothner semantic-prompts spec; ghostty `shell-integration/zsh/ghostty-integration`, `bash/ghostty.bash`, `Parser.zig`, `osc.zig`, `osc/parsers/semantic_prompt.zig`; Warp SSH docs |
| Resize: confirm flow `ioctl(TIOCSWINSZ)` + SIGWINCH client→host | **Confirmed.** Flow: (1) Client phát hiện resize (surface báo cols/rows mới); (2) Client gửi cols/rows tới host qua control channel; (3) **Host** gọi `ioctl(master_fd, TIOCSWINSZ, &winsize)`; (4) Kernel atomically update winsize struct + gửi SIGWINCH tới foreground process group của controlling terminal (slave side); (5) Shell/TUI nhận SIGWINCH, `ioctl(TIOCGWINSZ)` đọc size mới, redraw. `ioctl(TIOCSWINSZ)` CHỈ ở host; client KHÔNG bao giờ gọi kernel — chỉ thông báo dimensions. Giống hệt SSH window-change. | SwiftTerm `Pty.swift:117-123`, `MacLocalTerminalView.swift:97-104`; rkoucha SIGWINCH ref; cmux issue #2588 |
| `kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl` (+HEVC Apple Silicon), `EnableLTR`, `ForceLTRRefresh` (CFBoolean vs CFNumber), `AllowOpenGOP` default, `MaxFrameDelayCount` semantics — confirm exact symbols + availability | **Confirmed từ SDK header (verify trên MacOSX26.5.sdk, symbol không đổi từ 15.4):** (1) `EnableLowLatencyRateControl`: `macos(11.3)/ios(14.5)/tvos(14.5)/visionos(1.0)`, CFBoolean. Intel = H.264-only; Apple Silicon (ARM64) hỗ trợ HEVC (FFmpeg condition `H264 \|\| (TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && HEVC)` tại `videotoolboxenc.c:1714-1715`). Enforce Infinite GOP / no reorder / High profiles only. (2) `EnableLTR`: `macos(12.0)/ios(15.0)`, CFBoolean. (3) `ForceLTRRefresh`: `macos(12.0)/ios(15.0)` — header **mâu thuẫn**: type annotation = `CFNumberRef` nhưng docstring nói "Set to kCFBooleanTrue" (xem mục B). (4) `AllowOpenGOP`: `macos(10.14)/ios(12.0)`, default HEVC = kCFBooleanTrue (encoder MAY dùng open GOP), set kCFBooleanFalse để đảm bảo independently-decodable GOP. (5) `MaxFrameDelayCount`: `macos(10.8)/ios(8.0)`, CFNumber<int>; nếu = M thì trước khi encode frame N return, frame N-M phải đã emit; default `kVTUnlimitedFrameDelayCount` (-1); set 0 = zero-latency 1-in-1-out. **Lưu ý quirk**: query `UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder` sau khi set LLR có thể trả `kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr` (forum 751291) — KHÔNG confirm được đây có nghĩa vẫn dùng HW hay không (xem B); đừng dựa vào property này để verify HW, dùng `VTCopyVideoEncoderList` hoặc Instruments. | `VTCompressionProperties.h` (MacOSX SDK) các dòng symbol nêu trên; FFmpeg `videotoolboxenc.c:1714-1715,63-64`; Apple forum thread 751291 |
| `SCFrameStatus.idle`: ScreenCaptureKit có tiếp tục deliver callback ở cadence `minimumFrameInterval` khi idle, hay suppress? | **SCStream VẪN deliver callback ở (xấp xỉ) cadence `minimumFrameInterval` ngay cả khi nội dung không đổi**, sample buffer mang status `SCFrameStatusIdle` (=1, "new frame was not generated because the display did not change"); IOSurface trong buffer là surface cũ (không phải null). Forum thread 718356 xác nhận "rất nhiều frame idle dù không có gì đổi". → **Hệ quả thiết kế encode thread**: KHÔNG được sleep encode thread dựa trên vắng callback. NÊN inspect `SCStreamFrameInfoStatus` attachment và SKIP submit VideoToolbox cho frame idle để tiết kiệm GPU/media-engine. Pattern đúng: callback luôn fire ~minimumFrameInterval → check status → skip encode nếu idle. Idle callbacks có thể bị jitter. | `SCStream.h:34-51`; OBS `mac-sck-common.m:213-316`; Apple forum 718356; Apple docs SCFrameStatus.idle |

## B. CHỈ SPIKE MỚI BIẾT (SPIKE-ONLY)

Tất cả `status=spike-only` — không thể trả lời bằng desk-research, cần đo/prototype trên thiết bị thật.

| Câu hỏi | Đo cái gì, đo như thế nào | Gate cái gì |
|---|---|---|
| `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowTemporalCompression=false` (all-intra) có ép HEVC software-encode trên Apple Silicon không? | Tạo `VTCompressionSession` cho HEVC với **đồng thời** `AllowTemporalCompression=false` **và** `RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=true` trên thiết bị M-series, check return code của `VTCompressionSessionCreate` (succeed → HW giữ; error → config không được HW hỗ trợ). Header không doc hành vi HW/SW cho property này; FFmpeg KHÔNG set property này (đạt all-intra qua `MaxKeyFrameInterval=1` tại `videotoolboxenc.c:1388`). | Gate quyết định **cách đạt all-intra HEVC**: dùng `AllowTemporalCompression=false` (chưa rõ HW) hay theo FFmpeg dùng `MaxKeyFrameInterval=1` (đã battle-tested, an toàn HW). Nếu spike fail → bắt buộc dùng `MaxKeyFrameInterval=1`. |
| `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ConstantBitRate` có được HEVC hỗ trợ ổn định trên mọi OS version Apple Silicon không? (header cho phép macos(13)/ios(16) nhưng có report `kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr` trên 1 số iOS) | Probe `VTSessionCopySupportedPropertyDictionary` (hoặc set ConstantBitRate rồi bắt `kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr` như FFmpeg `videotoolboxenc.c:1269-1276`) cho HEVC trên TỪNG target OS/device. Forum thread 729545 KHÔNG nói rõ codec người báo lỗi đang dùng → phải tự đo HEVC riêng. KHÔNG dùng chung với `EnableLowLatencyRateControl`. | Gate **rate-control mode**: nếu CBR không khả dụng cho HEVC trên OS đích → fallback `AverageBitRate` + `DataRateLimits`. |
| `kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceLTRRefresh` nên truyền CFBoolean hay CFNumber-wrapping-1? (header mâu thuẫn: type annotation `CFNumberRef` nhưng docstring nói "kCFBooleanTrue") | Thử cả 2 dạng (kCFBooleanTrue và CFNumber(1)) trên encoder thật, kiểm tra LTR refresh có thực sự được force (quan sát frame type / LTR behavior trong bitstream). | Gate **cách trigger LTR refresh** — sai kiểu có thể bị encoder ignore silently. |
| `mach_timebase` numer/denom trên M2/M3/M4 — giống M1 (125/3) hay khác? | Chạy `mach_timebase_info(&info); printf("%u/%u", info.numer, info.denom)` trên TỪNG chip generation. Kỳ vọng 125/3 (24 MHz reference, ARM mandate fixed CNTFRQ_EL0) nhưng device tree freq không nằm trong XNU open-source → không confirm được qua đọc source. **Luôn gọi API, KHÔNG hardcode.** | Gate **timestamp conversion** (tick→ns) cho presentation timestamp / latency budget. Sai sẽ lệch toàn bộ timing nếu hardcode 125/3. |
| Bitrate target cho terminal text đọc được (pre-marked spike-only) | Đo VMAF/SSIM thực tế ở 1–4 Mbps cho 4K terminal text @60fps với HEVC trên thiết bị đích; tìm ngưỡng perceptual chữ vẫn sắc nét. | Gate **bitrate budget** của GUI-window video path. |
| 4:2:0 chroma subsampling fringing có chấp nhận được không (pre-marked spike-only) | Render terminal text qua full pipeline encode→decode→display, đánh giá color fringing trên chữ sáng/nền tối ở độ phân giải display đích. Cần đánh giá perceptual trên hardware thật. | Gate **chroma format**: 4:2:0 (nhẹ, HW-friendly) vs 4:4:4 (sắc text, tốn hơn). |

## C. CẦN BẠN QUYẾT ĐỊNH (DECISION-NEEDED)

### Quyết định 1 — Giá trị `TERM` cho Claude Code chạy qua remote PTY

**Câu hỏi:** Đặt `TERM` thành gì? Hai bug đang active kéo ngược chiều nhau: `xterm-ghostty` cho kitty keyboard + DEC 2026 auto-detect nhưng dính bug paste-tokenization (#54700, đóng "not planned", chưa fix) làm mỗi newline trong multi-line paste biến thành Enter; `xterm-256color` an toàn nhưng mất kitty keyboard + mất DEC 2026 auto-detect.

- **Option A — `TERM=xterm-ghostty`:** Được kitty keyboard protocol (Shift+Enter, Cmd+C, Ctrl+J native), DEC 2026 auto-detect. *Tradeoff:* bug #54700 multi-line paste mangle newline thành submit — rủi ro CAO cho coding tool. Vẫn cần `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1` nếu TERM bị override upstream.
- **Option B — `TERM=xterm-256color`:** Paste hoạt động đúng (workaround confirmed trong #54700). *Tradeoff:* DEC 2026 KHÔNG auto-detect (phải set `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1`); kitty keyboard không bật → Shift+Enter / extended modifier / Cmd+C fallback về xterm chuẩn. Baseline an toàn.
- **Option C — Custom TERM string riêng:** Nếu không nằm trong allowlist hardcode của CC (chỉ `xterm-ghostty`, `xterm-kitty`) thì mất DEC 2026 auto-detect bất kể gì, nhưng tránh được kitty paste path. Cần `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1`. Thực chất giống Option B nhưng thêm confusion.
- **Option D — `xterm-ghostty` + theo dõi fix #54700:** Chỉ bật `xterm-ghostty` khi Anthropic fix paste tokenizer; trong lúc chờ default về Option B + cho user 1 toggle.

**Khuyến nghị research:** Chọn **Option B (`xterm-256color`) + `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1`** làm default, kèm toggle bật `xterm-ghostty` (Option D) cho user nào cần kitty keyboard và chấp nhận rủi ro paste. Lý do: với coding tool, paste multi-line đúng là non-negotiable; bug #54700 đang "not planned"/chưa fix nên không thể dựa vào. DEC 2026 vẫn lấy được qua `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1`, chỉ mất kitty keyboard — chấp nhận được so với mất paste. *Nguồn:* issues #54700, #49584, #55613; docs env-vars; docs terminal-config.

> ⚠ Open question liên quan: tập đầy đủ TERM values trong allowlist DEC 2026 của CC ngoài `xterm-ghostty`/`xterm-kitty` chưa rõ; và nếu remote PTY chạy Claude Code **bên trong tmux** thì `CLAUDE_CODE_FORCE_SYNC_OUTPUT=1` "has no effect under tmux" → synchronized output không khả dụng bất kể TERM. Cần xác định tmux có trong kiến trúc không.

### Quyết định 2 — Mô hình Auth / Billing

**Câu hỏi:** Dùng API-key (`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, pay-as-you-go) hay subscription/OAuth (từ 2026-06-15 có Agent SDK credit riêng theo plan)? Cả hai đều fully supported; đây là lựa chọn kiến trúc. Precedence: cloud provider envs > `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` > `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` > apiKeyHelper > `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` > subscription OAuth (`/login`).

- **Option A — `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (pay-as-you-go):** Độc lập với subscription cá nhân, rotate qua Console không ảnh hưởng session, billing tách bạch. Không có monthly credit — mọi token tính giá API. Tốt cho team-owned automation / CI / multi-user. *Cảnh báo:* nếu vừa có API key vừa có OAuth thì API key thắng → auth fail nếu key hết hạn/disabled.
- **Option B — Subscription OAuth (`/login` hoặc `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN`):** Dùng credential claude.ai. Interactive session rút từ subscription limit. Từ 2026-06-15, `claude -p` + Agent SDK rút từ **monthly Agent SDK credit** riêng (Pro $20 / Max5x $100 / Max20x $200). `claude setup-token` tạo token 1 năm cho headless. Chỉ inference, không lập được Remote Control session. Tốt cho dev cá nhân gắn personal plan.
- **Option C — Hybrid:** Interactive dùng OAuth, CI/automation dùng API key. Billing tách sạch nhưng phải quản 2 credential path; phải unset `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` trong interactive shell để OAuth không bị override.
- **Option D — `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` long-lived:** Token 1 năm qua `claude setup-token`, auth như subscription, ăn Agent SDK credit, chạy được headless CI không cần browser login. *Lưu ý:* KHÔNG hoạt động với `--bare` (bare mode cần API key/apiKeyHelper).

**Khuyến nghị research:** Vì đây là **remote-coding tool dùng hằng ngày để chạy Claude Code interactive cho 1 người** → chọn **Option B (Subscription OAuth)**, dùng **`claude setup-token` (Option D)** để có token 1 năm cho host daemon headless. Chỉ chuyển sang Option A/C nếu sản phẩm thành team/multi-user với automation/CI tách biệt. *Nguồn:* docs authentication; support article Agent SDK credit.

> ⚠ Open question: ranh giới billing cho mixed session (interactive nhưng gọi headless sub-agent qua Agent SDK trong 1 tool call) chưa được doc đầy đủ.

Đã phản ánh inline. Hai câu pre-marked spike-only (bitrate, 4:2:0 fringing) giữ nguyên ở mục B. `AllowTemporalCompression` và `ConstantBitRate` cho HEVC được verification xác nhận đúng là **uncertain/spike-only** (header cho phép symbol nhưng hành vi HW runtime chỉ đo được trên device).

corpus.summary: All five questions resolved by reading actual source. Key findings: (1) Rendering is opaque — no parsed escape-sequence stream is exposed through the public C API, but OSC 133 command-finished events surface indirectly via GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED; (2) Alt-screen (DECSET 1049) works correctly because processOutput feeds raw bytes through ghostty's own terminal_stream VT parser; (3) Ghostty performs all kitty-keyboard-protocol encoding itself inside encodeKey()/key_encode.encode() — the host just passes ghostty_input_key_s structs; (4) ghostty_surface_write_buffer / processOutput acquires renderer_state.mutex internally and is thread-safe from any thread, but VVTerm and Lakr233 call it from the main actor as a convention; (5) InMemoryTerminalSession is Lakr233's production wrapper — it uses GHOSTTY_SURFACE_IO_BACKEND_HOST_MANAGED (the upstream patch in 0002-host-managed-io.patch), bridges receive(Data) to ghostty_surface_write_buffer, and exposes write+resize callbacks for the host side.

resolutions.question: Does the external-backend / full-surface API expose a PARSED escape-sequence / terminal-state stream to the host app, or ONLY a rendered surface (Metal/pixels)? Can the host read OSC 133 / cursor / cell grid for a client-side block UI?
resolutions.answer: Rendering and VT parsing are fully opaque inside libghostty — there is NO API that streams parsed escape sequences or exposes raw cursor/cell-grid structs to the host. The public C API in ghostty.h offers only two ways to read terminal content: (a) ghostty_surface_read_text(surface, selection, &out) and ghostty_surface_read_selection(surface, &out), which return UTF-8 text snapshots of an arbitrary viewport region; (b) the action callback system (ghostty_surface_callback_read_clipboard, GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE, GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED, GHOSTTY_ACTION_PROGRESS_REPORT, etc.) which fire when the terminal parses specific sequences. OSC 133 shell-integration is fully processed internally by ghostty's stream_terminal.zig and surfaces only through GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED (exit code + duration_ns) — the host never sees the raw OSC 133 bytes. Cursor position and the full cell grid are NOT exposed as structured data. For a client-side block UI (e.g. knowing where the prompt ends), the host must use ghostty_surface_read_text() to read viewport text and infer structure, or rely on the action callbacks for OSC 133 events. VVTerm's Ghostty.Surface.swift and Lakr233's InMemoryTerminalSession both confirm this: they use ghostty_surface_read_text + the action bridge (TerminalCallbackBridge.handleAction) with no cell-grid API.

resolutions.question: Does alt-screen switch (DECSET 1049 smcup/rmcup) work correctly when bytes are fed via the external-backend feed function + rendered by libghostty (fullscreen TUI like vim/Claude-Code-fullscreen)?
resolutions.answer: Yes, alt-screen works correctly. When bytes are fed via ghostty_surface_write_buffer (Lakr233/wiedymi) or ghostty_surface_write_output (daiimus), they go directly into Termio.processOutput(), which calls terminal_stream.nextSlice(buf) — the same full VT parser pipeline used by the PTY exec backend. The terminal state machine in Terminal.zig fully implements DECSET 1049 (mode alt_screen_save_cursor_clear_enter at value 1049) with save-cursor + clear + enter-alt-screen semantics. The ghostty source at src/terminal/modes.zig:294 defines the mode, and Terminal.zig:3097-3105 implements the cursor-save and alt-screen entry. The entire VT parser runs identically regardless of backend — the external/host-managed backend is just a different I/O source feeding the same byte stream to the same parser. Fullscreen TUI apps (vim, Claude Code fullscreen) will behave identically to the exec backend.
sources: ghostty-org/ghostty src/termio/Termio.zig:643-649 (processOutput calls terminal_stream.nextSlice — same parser for all backends)
ghostty-org/ghostty src/Terminal.zig:3097-3105 (cursor copy + alt-screen enter for mode 1049)

resolutions.question: Keyboard: how does libghostty surface turn key events into bytes emitted via the write-callback — does it implement kitty keyboard protocol encoding itself, or must the host encode?
resolutions.answer: Ghostty implements ALL keyboard encoding itself. The host passes a ghostty_input_key_s struct (action, mods, consumed_mods, keycode, text, unshifted_codepoint, composing) to ghostty_surface_key(), which routes through App.keyEvent() → Surface.encodeKey() → input.key_encode.encode(). The encoder reads the live terminal state — kitty_flags from t.screens.active.kitty_keyboard.current(), cursor_key_application mode, modify_other_keys_state_2, alt_esc_prefix, etc. — and emits the correct byte sequence for the negotiated encoding. This means Shift+Enter, Option+Enter, Ctrl+Alt+keys, all kitty progressive enhancement sequences are produced by ghostty's input/key_encode.zig and input/kitty.zig. The host is responsible ONLY for: (1) providing the raw platform key event as a ghostty_input_key_s with correct mods and text; (2) NOT encoding the bytes itself. The write-callback (GHOSTTY_SURFACE_IO_BACKEND_HOST_MANAGED's receive_buffer, or the External backend's write_callback) receives the already-encoded bytes that should be sent to the remote PTY. Lakr233's UITerminalView+Keyboard.swift confirms: pressesBegan builds a ghostty_input_key_s and calls surface.sendKeyEvent(), never encoding bytes manually.

ghostty-org/ghostty src/input/key_encode.zig:1-73 (Options struct: kitty_flags, modify_other_keys, alt_esc_prefix all read from terminal state)
ghostty-org/ghostty src/input/kitty.zig (kitty keyboard protocol encoding table)
ghostty-org/ghostty include/ghostty.h:324-330 (ghostty_input_key_s struct — what the host passes in)
Lakr233/libghostty-spm AGENTS.md (iOS Input Architecture: hardware keys → pressesBegan → builds ghostty_input_key_s → surface.sendKeyEvent())

resolutions.question: Thread-safety: from which thread/queue must ghostty_surface_feed_data / ghostty_surface_write_buffer be called?
resolutions.answer: ghostty_surface_write_buffer (Lakr233/wiedymi patch) and ghostty_surface_write_output (daiimus fork) are both internally thread-safe: they call Termio.processOutput() which immediately acquires renderer_state.mutex before touching any terminal state. The daiimus fork documents this explicitly at src/apprt/embedded.zig:1961-1963: 'Thread safety: This function is NOT safe to call concurrently on the same surface. The caller must serialize calls for a given surface. It IS safe to call concurrently on different surfaces.' In practice this means: calls to write_buffer/write_output for a single surface must be serialized (one at a time), but can come from any thread — they do not need to be on the main thread. VVTerm's feedData() is marked @MainActor, and Lakr233's receive() is called without an explicit actor annotation (it uses NSLock internally for surface pointer protection). The renderer_state.mutex inside processOutput provides the actual VT-parser-state protection. For a remote-coding tool streaming SSH/PTY bytes, calling write_buffer from a dedicated I/O thread (not the main thread) is correct and intended — just ensure serial dispatch per surface.
sources: ghostty-org/ghostty src/termio/Termio.zig:643-648 (processOutput acquires renderer_state.mutex before any terminal state access)
daiimus/ghostty src/apprt/embedded.zig:1961-1965 (explicit thread-safety doc for ghostty_surface_write_output)
vivy-company/vvterm VVTerm/GhosttyTerminal/Ghostty.Surface.swift:213 (@MainActor annotation on feedData — VVTerm convention, not a libghostty requirement)
Lakr233/libghostty-spm Sources/GhosttyTerminal/InMemory/InMemoryTerminalSession.swift:126-148 (receive() uses NSLock for pointer guard, no main-thread requirement)

resolutions.question: What EXACTLY does Lakr233 InMemoryTerminalSession provide and does it carry the external-backend patch or plain upstream?
resolutions.answer: InMemoryTerminalSession is Lakr233's Swift abstraction over the host-managed I/O backend. It is NOT plain upstream ghostty — it requires the patch in Patches/ghostty/0002-host-managed-io.patch which adds GHOSTTY_SURFACE_IO_BACKEND_HOST_MANAGED, ghostty_surface_write_buffer, and ghostty_surface_process_exit to the ghostty C API (modifying include/ghostty.h, src/Surface.zig, src/apprt/embedded.zig, src/termio.zig, and adding src/termio/HostManaged.zig). The patch README explicitly states the goal: 'host-managed IO work required for sandboxed iOS, macOS, and Mac Catalyst integration'. InMemoryTerminalSession provides: (1) receive(Data) / receive(String) — calls ghostty_surface_write_buffer to feed server bytes into the terminal parser; (2) write callback — a Swift closure called when user input bytes should be sent to the host (the receiveBufferCallback C function unwraps the session and calls writeHandler); (3) resize callback — called when terminal grid size changes (receiveResizeCallback); (4) finish(exitCode:runtimeMilliseconds:) — calls ghostty_surface_process_exit to simulate process exit; (5) readViewportText() — reads the current viewport as a UTF-8 string via ghostty_surface_read_text. Input handling (keyboard → terminal bytes) is entirely separate: the host calls surface.sendKeyEvent(ghostty_input_key_s) and ghostty encodes the bytes, which then fire the write callback. The session does NOT expose a parsed VT state stream — same limitation as Q1.

Lakr233/libghostty-spm Patches/ghostty/README.md ('host-managed IO work required for sandboxed iOS, macOS, and Mac Catalyst integration')
Lakr233/libghostty-spm AGENTS.md:74-75 ('Use GHOSTTY_SURFACE_IO_BACKEND_HOST_MANAGED for non-PTY I/O. Never disable sandbox or spawn subprocesses.')

verdict.evidence:
The claim is confirmed with one important correction and one important nuance that the original claim missed.

**Confirmed parts (all backed by source)**

1. `ghostty.h` (surface embedding API) exposes exactly the two read paths the claim describes:
   - `ghostty_surface_read_text(surface, selection, &out)` and `ghostty_surface_read_selection(surface, &out)` — UTF-8 text snapshots of a viewport region ([daiimus/ghostty include/ghostty.h:1217–1221](https://github.com/daiimus/ghostty/blob/ios-external-backend/include/ghostty.h)).
   - The action-callback system via `ghostty_runtime_action_cb`: `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PWD`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PROGRESS_REPORT`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SCROLLBAR`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_CELL_SIZE`, etc. ([ghostty.h:960–1000](https://github.com/daiimus/ghostty/blob/ios-external-backend/include/ghostty.h)).

2. OSC 133 is fully parsed internally. In `src/termio/stream_handler.zig`, the `.semantic_prompt` handler calls `try self.semanticPrompt(value)` (line 333) and `.end_command` dispatches `.stop_command` via `surfaceMessageWriter` (line 1092). `src/Surface.zig` converts `.stop_command` into a `performAction(.command_finished, ...)` call (lines 1140–1149). The host never sees raw OSC 133 bytes.

3. No cursor-position or cell-grid API exists in `ghostty.h`. A grep for `cursor_pos`, `cell_grid`, `cell_content`, `parsed_sequence`, `escape_sequence` across the full 1369-line daiimus `ghostty.h` returns zero matches.

4. `TerminalCallbackBridge.handleAction` in `Lakr233/libghostty-spm` handles exactly: `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_CELL_SIZE`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_RING_BELL`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_RENDER`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_CONFIG_CHANGE`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PROGRESS_REPORT`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_DESKTOP_NOTIFICATION`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_OPEN_URL`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_MOUSE_OVER_LINK`, `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PWD` — no cell-grid or cursor API ([TerminalCallbackBridge.swift:28–151](https://github.com/Lakr233/libghostty-spm/blob/main/Sources/GhosttyTerminal/InMemory/TerminalCallbackBridge.swift)).

5. `InMemoryTerminalSession.readViewportText()` uses `ghostty_surface_read_text` with `GHOSTTY_POINT_VIEWPORT` top-left to bottom-right selection — exactly the text-snapshot approach described. There is no cell-grid read anywhere ([InMemoryTerminalSession.swift:74–109](https://github.com/Lakr233/libghostty-spm/blob/main/Sources/GhosttyTerminal/InMemory/InMemoryTerminalSession.swift)).

6. VVTerm's bridging header explicitly blocks `vt.h`: `// Do NOT include ghostty/vt.h as it causes duplicate enum definitions` ([ghostty-bridging-header.h:13](https://github.com/vivy-company/vvterm/blob/main/VVTerm/ghostty-bridging-header.h)).

**Important correction the original claim missed**

`ghostty-org/ghostty` ships a second, entirely separate C library: `libghostty-vt` (exposed via `include/ghostty/vt.h`). This library DOES expose:
- Full cell-grid traversal via `ghostty_terminal_grid_ref()` ([vt/grid_ref.h](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/blob/main/include/ghostty/vt/grid_ref.h))
- Cursor position read via `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_CURSOR_X`, `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_CURSOR_Y`, `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_CURSOR_VISIBLE` ([vt/terminal.h:645–700](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/blob/main/include/ghostty/vt/terminal.h))
- A `ghostty_terminal_vt_write()` API for injecting raw VT bytes and effect callbacks for OSC title-change, bell, etc.

However, `libghostty-vt` is NOT the surface embedding API. It is a standalone VT-emulator library that the host must own and drive independently (create with `ghostty_terminal_new`, feed bytes with `ghostty_terminal_vt_write`). It has NO connection to `ghostty_surface_t` — you cannot call `ghostty_terminal_grid_ref` on a surface. The vt.h header is also explicitly excluded from vvterm's bridging header and not used by Lakr233/libghostty-spm, confirming it is not part of the surface embedding path.

**Summary table of the surface embedding API (`ghostty.h` only)**

| What host can read | API |
|---|---|
| Viewport text (UTF-8 snapshot) | `ghostty_surface_read_text` / `ghostty_surface_read_selection` |
| OSC 133 command end | `GHOSTTY_ACTION_COMMAND_FINISHED` (exit_code + duration_ns) |
| OSC 7 working dir | `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PWD` |
| Window title (OSC 0/2) | `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SET_TITLE` |
| Progress (OSC 9;4) | `GHOSTTY_ACTION_PROGRESS_REPORT` |
| Cell dimensions (px) | `GHOSTTY_ACTION_CELL_SIZE` |
| Scrollbar metrics | `GHOSTTY_ACTION_SCROLLBAR` |
| Cursor position | NOT EXPOSED |
| Raw cell grid | NOT EXPOSED |
| Raw VT/escape bytes | NOT EXPOSED |
verdict.correction: The original claim is substantively correct but omits a critical nuance: there IS a cell-grid and cursor-position C API in the ghostty source tree, but it lives in the separate `libghostty-vt` library (`include/ghostty/vt.h`), NOT in the surface embedding API (`include/ghostty.h`). `libghostty-vt` exposes `ghostty_terminal_grid_ref()`, `ghostty_terminal_grid_ref_track()`, and cursor-position reads via `GHOSTTY_TERMINAL_DATA_CURSOR_X/Y/VISIBLE` on a standalone `GhosttyTerminal` handle that is completely independent from `ghostty_surface_t`. The claim should be revised to say: 'Cursor position and the full cell grid are NOT exposed through the surface embedding API (`ghostty.h`). They ARE exposed by the separate `libghostty-vt` library (`vt.h`), which the host must own and drive independently — there is no bridge between a `ghostty_surface_t` and the `libghostty-vt` terminal model.' All three downstream consumers (vvterm, Lakr233/libghostty-spm, daiimus fork) use `ghostty.h` exclusively and do not use the vt.h cell-grid APIs in their surface integration paths."

All three feed functions were traced to the identical processOutput call:

1. ghostty_surface_write_buffer (Lakr233 patch 0002-host-managed-io.patch:235-242): `surface.core_surface.io.processOutput(ptr[0..len])` — confirmed in the patch diff at line 241.

2. ghostty_surface_write_output (daiimus ios-external-backend, src/apprt/embedded.zig:1966-1973): `surface.core_surface.io.processOutput(ptr[0..len])` — the assert ensures the surface uses the external backend.

3. ghostty_surface_feed_data (wiedymi custom-io, src/apprt/embedded.zig:2264-2271): `ptr.core_surface.io.processOutput(data[0..len])` — identical path.

processOutput (daiimus src/termio/Termio.zig:764-769) acquires the renderer_state mutex and delegates to processOutputLocked, which calls `self.terminal_stream.nextSlice(buf)` at line 814 — the same VT parser path used by the Exec (PTY) backend.

The stream_handler wires mode 1049 to switchScreenMode: daiimus src/termio/stream_handler.zig:1093-1094 and ghostty-org src/termio/stream_handler.zig:707-708 both have `.alt_screen_save_cursor_clear_enter => try self.terminal.switchScreenMode(.@"1049", enabled)`.

switchScreenMode (ghostty-org src/terminal/Terminal.zig:3046-3115) implements full DECSET 1049 semantics: saveCursor() unconditionally on enable (line 3068), switchScreen to .alternate (line 3073), eraseDisplay(.complete) on entry (line 3095), cursorCopy from old primary (lines 3100-3108), and restoreCursor() on exit (line 3112). This matches xterm's srm_OPT_ALTBUF_CURSOR behavior (documented in the source comment).

modes.zig:294 defines `.alt_screen_save_cursor_clear_enter` with value 1049 — confirmed exact line.

One inaccuracy in the claim: the cited range "Terminal.zig:3097-3105 implements the cursor-save and alt-screen entry" is slightly off. The saveCursor() call is at line 3068 (before switchScreen), not in the 3097-3105 range. Lines 3097-3105 contain the cursorCopy step (copying primary screen cursor position to alt screen on entry), not saveCursor itself. The functional description is correct, but the line numbers mis-attribute which step is at 3097-3105.

corpus.summary: All four PTY-host + transport questions are fully resolvable from source code and authoritative documentation. TCP's ordered lossless delivery means simple accumulate-until-complete-frame buffering is sufficient; no loss-recovery layer is needed. forkpty() is explicitly unsafe in Swift (fork-without-exec breaks the runtime), so openpty() + posix_spawn(POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID) is the canonical safe pattern — confirmed by Apple DTS and implemented in both SwiftTerm and swiftlang/swift-subprocess. OSC 133 is purely a shell-hook byte-stream annotation with zero network requirements; it works identically over our TCP pipe because the real login shell emits the sequences before they reach the transport. Resize is a pure host-side ioctl: client sends new cols/rows over the control channel, host calls ioctl(TIOCSWINSZ) on the master PTY fd, the kernel atomically updates the winsize struct and sends SIGWINCH to the foreground process group — no client-side kernel call is involved.

resolutions.question: TCP byte-stream: does in-order lossless delivery mean we only need simple buffering/reassembly (accumulate until complete frame), NOT loss recovery?
resolutions.answer: Yes. TCP guarantees that every byte is delivered exactly once, in send order, with no losses (short of catastrophic link failure — handled by TCP retransmit, invisible to the application). A single recv() call MAY return fewer bytes than were sent in one write() — the kernel may coalesce or split at any MSS boundary. This means an OSC sequence can arrive split across two reads. The only required mechanism is a receive buffer that accumulates bytes until a complete escape sequence is terminated (ST '\033\\' or BEL '\007' for OSC; ESC ST for DCS, etc.). There is no need for sequence numbers, ACKs, or any application-level loss recovery. The framing contract is: buffer incoming bytes, scan for terminal characters, only dispatch complete sequences. This is identical to how every local PTY reader works (e.g. SwiftTerm's LocalProcess.swift reads with DispatchIO at up to 128 KB per callback with no loss logic).
sources: https://book.systemsapproach.org/e2e/tcp.html — 'TCP is a reliable byte stream delivery service; all bytes received will be identical and in the same order as those sent'
https://dev.to/min38/tcp-variable-length-packet-handling-pc — 'recv() returns as much data as is currently available, from a minimum of 1 byte to the maximum requested size'

resolutions.question: openpty() + posix_spawn(POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID, login_tty) vs forkpty() for the host PTY bridge on macOS — which is the safe pattern?
resolutions.answer: openpty() + posix_spawn with POSIX_SPAWN_SETSID (or the equivalent setsid() shim) is the correct safe pattern. forkpty() is explicitly unsafe in Swift: it performs fork() without an immediate exec(), leaving the Swift runtime (which depends on malloc mutexes) in an indeterminate state in the child process. Apple DTS confirmed this in developer forum thread 130254: 'The forkpty call is not safe to use from Swift. It basically involves a fork without an exec, and it's not safe to run Swift code in that environment.' The correct approach is: (1) call openpty(&master, &slave, nil, nil, &winsize) to allocate the PTY pair; (2) use posix_spawn (or swiftlang/swift-subprocess with PlatformOptions.createSession = true, which internally calls setsid() in the child before exec) to launch the shell, passing the slave fd as stdin/stdout/stderr; (3) close the slave fd in the parent. The createSession flag in swift-subprocess sources/_SubprocessCShims/process_shims.c lines ~200-210 shows the exact pattern: it forks, calls setsid() in the child, then posix_spawns with POSIX_SPAWN_SETEXEC. SwiftTerm's own comment in LocalProcess.swift:61 reads 'This implementation uses swift-subprocess with openpty/login_tty for pseudo-terminal support' and its active (non-guarded) code path at line 97 uses forkpty() only via the pure-C PseudoTerminalHelpers.fork() wrapper — the Swift calling code never crosses fork() itself. For a host daemon written purely in Swift, use openpty() + swiftlang/swift-subprocess with createSession:true and preSpawnProcessConfigurator to dup2 slave fd to stdin/stdout/stderr before exec.

resolutions.question: Does OSC 133 shell integration work over our setup — host runs a real login shell in a PTY, byte stream flows over TCP to client — is there anything network-specific blocking it?
resolutions.answer: OSC 133 works without any modification. The protocol is defined as pure byte-stream annotations: the shell's precmd/preexec hooks (zsh) or PROMPT_COMMAND/DEBUG trap (bash) emit OSC 133 A/B/C/D sequences directly to stdout via printf calls like printf '\033]133;A\007'. These bytes flow through the PTY master fd on the host, through the TCP connection, and arrive at the client's terminal emulator exactly as if they came from a local PTY. There is zero network-specific logic; no remote daemon, agent, or protocol extension is required. This contrasts with Warp over SSH: Warp needs its remote server extension only for proprietary features (file tree, native diffs) — its own documentation confirms that 'blocks, command history, autosuggestions, and history search behave the same as in a local session' without the extension, meaning OSC 133-derived block semantics work through the raw byte stream. Since our setup runs a real login shell (not a restricted subshell), shell hook files (~/.zshrc, /etc/zshrc, Ghostty's shell-integration scripts) execute normally and emit OSC 133 into the byte stream that we forward verbatim.
sources: https://contour-terminal.org/vt-extensions/osc-133-shell-integration/ — 'No agent required. Sequences flow from the shell through standard output to the terminal emulator. No need for background daemons or remote agents.'
https://docs.warp.dev/terminal/warpify/ssh/ — Warp SSH: blocks/history/autosuggestions work without remote extension; extension only needed for file tree and native diffs
https://deepwiki.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/9-shell-integration — Ghostty OSC 133 shell integration via shell hook injection

resolutions.question: Resize: confirm ioctl(TIOCSWINSZ) + SIGWINCH flow client->host
resolutions.answer: The flow is: (1) Client detects a window resize (e.g. the libghostty surface reports new cols/rows); (2) Client sends the new cols/rows to the host over the control channel (a separate TCP message or in-band escape, your choice); (3) Host calls ioctl(master_fd, TIOCSWINSZ, &winsize) on the PTY master fd — confirmed in SwiftTerm's PseudoTerminalHelpers.setWinSize() at Pty.swift:120 and MacLocalTerminalView.swift:102; (4) The kernel atomically updates the kernel-side winsize struct and delivers SIGWINCH to the foreground process group of the controlling terminal (the slave side of the PTY); (5) The shell or TUI app receives SIGWINCH, calls ioctl(TIOCGWINSZ) to read the new size, and redraws. The ioctl(TIOCSWINSZ) call is exclusively on the host — the client never needs to call it. The client's only job is to notify the host of the new dimensions; the host performs the ioctl. This is identical to how SSH resize works (SSH_MSG_CHANNEL_REQUEST window-change), and is the exact pattern SwiftTerm uses in its sizeChanged callback.

A single recv() call MAY return fewer bytes than were sent — the only required mechanism is a receive buffer that accumulates bytes until a complete escape sequence is terminated. verdict.evidence:
Four independent lines of evidence confirm every sub-claim.

**1. TCP specification — RFC 9293 (2022, current standard)**
RFC 9293 §1.1 states verbatim: "TCP provides a reliable, in-order, byte-stream service to applications." §3.8.1 defines reliability as detection of loss via sequence numbers and correction via retransmission — entirely inside the TCP layer, invisible to the application. RFC 793 (original 1981 spec, still authoritative) adds: "As long as the TCPs continue to function properly and the internet system does not become completely partitioned, no transmission errors will affect the correct delivery of data." Applications receive an ordered byte stream and are explicitly not required to perform any loss recovery.

**2. Partial-read behaviour — kernel TCP receive buffer**
A single recv()/read() call on a TCP socket returns however many bytes the kernel has in its receive buffer at that moment — this can be anywhere from 1 byte to the full socket receive window. The TCP layer has already reassembled out-of-order segments and retransmitted any lost segments before surfacing bytes to the application. The application therefore never sees gaps, duplicates, or reordering. The only framing work needed is accumulating bytes until a complete application-level message (here: a complete VT/OSC escape sequence) has arrived.

**3. SwiftTerm LocalProcess.swift — DispatchIO, no loss logic**
SwiftTerm/Sources/SwiftTerm/LocalProcess.swift (line 64): `let readSize = 128*1024`. Lines 383–386 and 484–486: `io.setLimit(lowWater: 1); io.setLimit(highWater: readSize); io.read(offset: 0, length: readSize, ...)`. The callback at `childProcessRead` (lines 239–289) passes whatever bytes arrived directly to `delegate?.dataReceived(slice: b[...])` — no sequence numbers, no loss detection, no application-level ACK. This is exactly what the claim asserts.

**4. Ghostty terminal stream — state-machine handles split sequences across reads**
ghostty/src/termio/Exec.zig lines 1302–1338: `var buf: [1024]u8 = undefined; while (true) { const n = posix.read(fd, &buf); ... termio.Termio.processOutput(io, buf[0..n]); }`. Each read hands an arbitrary-sized slice (potentially mid-sequence) to `processOutput`.

ghostty/src/termio/Termio.zig lines 643–693: `processOutput` locks the renderer state and calls `self.terminal_stream.nextSlice(buf)`.

ghostty/src/terminal/stream.zig lines 503–515 (comment is the smoking gun): "If we're not in the ground state then we process until we are. **This can happen if the last chunk of input put us in the middle of a control sequence.**" The parser's own state machine persists across read() calls — there is no external accumulator buffer needed, and no loss-recovery machinery. The parser's `state` field (Parser.zig line 15–30: `ground`, `osc_string`, `dcs_passthrough`, etc.) is exactly the "buffer" that holds partial sequences across arbitrary TCP recv() boundaries.

**Summary of what IS and IS NOT needed**
- Needed: a stateful VT parser (Ghostty's `Parser.zig` / SwiftTerm's equivalent) whose state persists between read() callbacks. Each read() hands whatever bytes arrived to `nextSlice()`; the parser continues from wherever it left off.
- NOT needed: sequence numbers, application-level ACKs, duplicate detection, reorder buffers, or any loss-recovery logic. TCP provides all of that transparently.

verdict.claim: OSC 133 works without any modification over a setup where the host runs a real login shell in a PTY, the byte stream flows over TCP to the client, and there is zero network-specific logic required. Shell hook files execute normally and emit OSC 133 into the byte stream that is forwarded verbatim. Warp's own documentation confirms that 'blocks, command history, autosuggestions, and history search behave the same as in a local session' without a remote extension, meaning OSC 133-derived block semantics work through the raw byte stream.
verdict.evidence:
**1. Protocol is defined as pure byte-stream annotations (no transport dependency)**

The [Per Bothner semantic-prompts specification](https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/Per_Bothner/specifications/blob/master/proposals/semantic-prompts.md) (the authoritative OSC 133 spec, referenced directly in Ghostty's source at `src/terminal/osc/parsers/semantic_prompt.zig:1`) defines all sequences — `133;A`, `133;B`, `133;C`, `133;D`, etc. — as pure OSC escape sequences in the VT byte stream. The spec contains zero mention of transport, network, or daemon requirements. The sequences are terminated by BEL (`\007` / `\a`) and are structurally identical to any other VT escape sequence. They travel through whatever carries the byte stream: local PTY fd, SSH channel, or TCP socket alike.

**2. Shell integration scripts write directly to the TTY fd, not to any daemon**

Ghostty's zsh integration ([`src/shell-integration/zsh/ghostty-integration`](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/blob/main/src/shell-integration/zsh/ghostty-integration)) uses `builtin print -nu $_ghostty_fd '\e]133;C\a'` (preexec) and `builtin print -nu $_ghostty_fd '\e]133;D;'$cmd_status'\a'` (precmd). The `_ghostty_fd` is opened with `sysopen -o cloexec -wu` directly to `/dev/tty` or falls back to stdout (fd 1). This write path produces bytes on the PTY master fd on the host — there is no side channel, no daemon call, no network-specific helper. The bash integration ([`src/shell-integration/bash/ghostty.bash`](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/blob/main/src/shell-integration/bash/ghostty.bash)) uses `builtin printf "\e]133;C;\a"` and `builtin printf "\e]133;D;%s;aid=%s\a"` — again plain writes to stdout/tty.

**3. Terminal parser processes OSC 133 as byte-stream events, no network awareness**

Ghostty's VT parser ([`src/terminal/Parser.zig`](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/blob/main/src/terminal/Parser.zig)) implements a state machine that processes one byte at a time via `pub fn next(self: *Parser, c: u8)`. The OSC parser ([`src/terminal/osc.zig`](https://github.com/ghostty-org/ghostty/blob/main/src/terminal/osc.zig)) transitions through state `@"133"` on receiving the digits `1`, `3`, `3`, then dispatches to `parsers.semantic_prompt.parse`. The parser is completely byte-source-agnostic: it does not know or care whether the bytes came from a local PTY, an SSH channel, or a TCP socket.

**4. Warp confirms core features (blocks, history, autosuggestions) work over SSH without a remote extension**

Warp's [SSH feature support docs](https://docs.warp.dev/code/ssh-feature-support) state: "The input editor, completions, blocks, history search, and more — work the same as they do locally." The [SSH extension page](https://docs.warp.dev/terminal/warpify/ssh) lists what the extension *adds* (file tree, native file reads, native code diffs, parallel completions) — blocks are not in that list. This confirms the claim's framing: Warp's proprietary remote extension is needed only for file-tree/diff features, not for OSC 133-derived block semantics.

**5. One nuance vs. the claim: Warp's implementation is NOT OSC 133**

The Warp blog describes Warp's block mechanism as using "a custom DCS (Device Control String)" with "encoded JSON metadata," not OSC 133. This means Warp's blocks are a proprietary Warp-specific protocol, while OSC 133 is the open standard. Both travel as byte-stream annotations and neither requires a remote daemon, but they are distinct protocols. The claim's parenthetical ("OSC 133-derived block semantics") is technically imprecise for Warp specifically — Warp uses its own DCS, not OSC 133. However, this imprecision does not affect the core claim's validity: OSC 133 sequences do flow transparently through PTY→TCP→client with no network-specific requirements.

**6. No byte-stripping in SSH**

SSH channels are 8-bit clean binary channels. OpenSSH does not strip, alter, or interpret escape sequences in the PTY data channel. The bytes `\x1b]133;C\x07` written to the host PTY master by the shell arrive at the SSH client's stdout verbatim, which then hands them to the terminal emulator's VT parser byte-by-byte.
verdict.correction: The claim is correct on the core technical question. One small imprecision: Warp's block feature uses a proprietary DCS-based JSON protocol, NOT OSC 133. Citing Warp as proof that "OSC 133-derived block semantics work through the raw byte stream" is technically a category error — Warp's blocks and OSC 133 are different protocols that happen to share the same transport-agnostic property. The correct evidence for OSC 133 transparency is the spec definition itself (pure VT escape sequences with no transport coupling) and the shell integration source code (direct TTY writes, no daemon intermediary).

corpus.summary: All five questions were researched from Apple SDK headers (MacOSX15.4.sdk VTCompressionProperties.h, SCStream.h), FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c (master branch), OBS Studio mac-sck-video-capture.m, XNU open-source rtclock.c, and Apple Developer Forum threads. Four questions are resolved; one (mach_timebase for M2/M3/M4) requires a one-line on-device measurement and is marked spike-only. resolutions.question: Is kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ConstantBitRate available for HEVC on Apple Silicon (vs only AverageBitRate+DataRateLimits)?
resolutions.answer: The key exists in the SDK and is declared available on macOS 13.0/iOS 16.0 for all codecs. However, the header explicitly states 'This is not supported in all encoders or in all encoder operating modes. kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr will be returned when this option is not supported.' (VTCompressionProperties.h:343-344). Empirically, at least one developer on iOS 16.4+ received kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr when trying to use it with HEVC (Apple Developer Forums thread 729545). FFmpeg's videotoolboxenc.c handles this exactly: it sets ConstantBitRate and, on kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr, logs an error and returns AVERROR_EXTERNAL (videotoolboxenc.c:1273-1276). Additionally, kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is explicitly incompatible with ConstantBitRate (header comment line 335). Bottom line: ConstantBitRate is available as a symbol (macos(13)/ios(16)), and the H.264 hardware encoder on Apple Silicon supports it, but the HEVC hardware encoder may return kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr depending on OS version — you must probe with VTSessionCopySupportedPropertyDictionary or catch the error. It should not be used with EnableLowLatencyRateControl.

resolutions.question: Does kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowTemporalCompression=false (all-intra) force HEVC to software encode on Apple Silicon?
resolutions.answer: The header declares AllowTemporalCompression as Read/Write, CFBoolean, Optional, defaults to true (VTCompressionProperties.h:169). No Apple documentation or authoritative forum post states that setting it to false forces software encoding for HEVC on Apple Silicon. FFmpeg's videotoolboxenc.c does NOT set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowTemporalCompression at all — it achieves all-intra by setting MaxKeyFrameInterval=1 via kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameInterval (videotoolboxenc.c:1388). The kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder flag can coexist with MaxKeyFrameInterval=1 in FFmpeg without any software-fallback path. The historical 2017 thread (thread/87047) reported early HEVC hardware encoder property issues, but those were for AverageBitRate and DataRateLimits, not AllowTemporalCompression. The Apple Silicon HEVC hardware encoder does support encoding all-I streams via MaxKeyFrameInterval, and there is no public evidence it falls back to software for AllowTemporalCompression=false. However, no authoritative source definitively confirms it stays hardware. This is a SPIKE-ONLY question if you need certainty: set AllowTemporalCompression=false with RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=true on an M-series device and check whether the session creation succeeds or returns an error.

resolutions.question: Confirm exact symbols + availability: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl with HEVC on Apple Silicon, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR, kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceLTRRefresh (CFBoolean vs CFNumber), AllowOpenGOP default, MaxFrameDelayCount semantics.
resolutions.answer:
All symbols confirmed from VTCompressionProperties.h (MacOSX15.4.sdk):

1. kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl: API_AVAILABLE(macos(11.3), ios(14.5), tvos(14.5), visionos(1.0)). Type: CFBoolean (Read/Write, Optional). On Intel Macs it was H.264-only. On Apple Silicon (ARM64) it supports HEVC — confirmed by FFmpeg patch: condition is `AV_CODEC_FLAG_LOW_DELAY && (codec == H264 || (TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && codec == HEVC))` (videotoolboxenc.c:1714-1715). The header doc says it enforces 'Infinite GOP (all P frames following the beginning IDR), no frame reordering, only High profiles'. Note: querying kVTCompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder after setting this flag may return kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr — this is a known API quirk, not a sign software encoding is used (forum thread/751291).

2. kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR: API_AVAILABLE(macos(12.0), ios(15.0), tvos(15.0), visionos(1.0)). Type: CFBoolean (Read/Write, Optional).

3. kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceLTRRefresh: API_AVAILABLE(macos(12.0), ios(15.0), tvos(15.0), visionos(1.0)). Type: CFNumberRef (NOT CFBoolean — the declaration comment explicitly says 'CFNumberRef, Optional', and the description says 'Set this option to kCFBooleanTrue' but the actual declared type in the header is CFNumberRef). Set to kCFBooleanTrue cast as CFNumber or a CFNumber wrapping 1.

4. kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP: API_AVAILABLE(macos(10.14), ios(12.0), tvos(12.0), visionos(1.0)). Default for HEVC is kCFBooleanTrue (encoder MAY use Open GOP; not guaranteed). Set to kCFBooleanFalse to guarantee independently decodable GOPs. Read/Write, CFBoolean, Optional.

5. kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount: API_AVAILABLE(macos(10.8), ios(8.0)). Type: CFNumber<int>, Read/Write, Optional. Semantics: if set to M, before the VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame call for frame N returns, frame N-M must already have been emitted (i.e., limits the encoder's lookahead/reorder window). Default is kVTUnlimitedFrameDelayCount (-1), meaning no limit. Setting to 0 means every encode call must immediately emit a compressed frame (zero-latency), which when combined with EnableLowLatencyRateControl ensures 1-in-1-out. The kVTCompressionPropertyKey_SuggestedLookAheadFrameCount property (macos(15.0)) is related: 'Value of this property must be less than or equal to MaxFrameDelayCount.'

resolutions.question: SCFrameStatus.idle — does ScreenCaptureKit keep delivering callbacks at minimumFrameInterval cadence during idle, or suppress them?
resolutions.answer:
SCStream DOES deliver callbacks at (approximately) minimumFrameInterval cadence even when the display content has not changed; the callback fires and the sample buffer carries SCFrameStatusIdle status. The SCStream.h header definition (SCFrameStatusIdle = 1) says 'new frame was not generated because the display did not change' — notably it says the callback arrives but no new frame was generated, meaning the IOSurface in the buffer is the previous surface (not updated), not a null. Apple Developer Forum thread/718356 confirms users 'get a lot of frames with SCFrameStatus idle, even though nothing changes', explicitly establishing callback delivery at cadence. OBS Studio's mac-sck-common.m implements this correctly: screen_stream_video_update() is called every time, checks CVPixelBufferGetIOSurface(), and skips the update only if frame_surface is NULL or the mutex lock fails (not based on status). The SCStream header also does NOT promise any delivery rate guarantee ('desired minimum time between frame updates') meaning idle callbacks may be slightly jittered.

Consequence for encode thread design: You cannot sleep the encode thread on absence of SCK callback. You CAN inspect the SCFrameStatus attachment (via SCStreamFrameInfoStatus key on the sample buffer's attachments array) and skip the encode/VideoToolbox submission for idle frames, saving GPU/media-engine work. The correct pattern is: callback always fires at ~minimumFrameInterval, check status, skip encoding if SCFrameStatusIdle. Do not try to sleep/wake the encode thread based on callbacks arriving.

resolutions.question: mach_timebase numer/denom on M2/M3/M4 — same as M1 (125/3) or different?
resolutions.answer: M1 is documented as numer=125, denom=3 (24 MHz reference clock, 1 tick = 125/3 ns ≈ 41.67 ns). The XNU rtclock.c computes numer/denom from the platform's timebase_freq_t passed by iBoot via PE_register_timebase_callback (osfmk/arm/rtclock.c:106-137); the actual frequency value comes from the device tree, which is not in the open-source XNU distribution. Every published measurement and ARM architectural standard (CNTFRQ_EL0 = 24 MHz on all Apple Silicon) points to 125/3 being stable across M1/M2/M3/M4 — the ARM architecture mandates the counter runs at a fixed frequency per SoC, and Apple has consistently used 24 MHz across all M-series chips. However, no public source provides confirmed M2/M3/M4 measurements; the eclecticlight.co author explicitly warned it 'could vary from model to model'. To measure: run `mach_timebase_info(&info); printf('%u/%u', info.numer, info.denom)` on each target device. Expected outcome: 125/3 on all current Apple Silicon, but always call the API and never hardcode.

resolutions.question: Bitrate target for readable terminal text (spike-only, pre-marked)
resolutions.answer: Measure actual VMAF/SSIM scores at 1-4 Mbps for 4K terminal text at 60 fps with HEVC on target device. No desk-research substitute for this perceptual threshold.

resolutions.question: 4:2:0 chroma subsampling fringing acceptability (spike-only, pre-marked)
resolutions.answer: Render terminal text through the full encode/decode/display pipeline and evaluate color fringing on bright-foreground/dark-background text at target display resolution. Perceptual evaluation required on real hardware.

The header states 'This is not supported in all encoders or in all encoder operating modes. kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr will be returned when this option is not supported.' Bottom line: ConstantBitRate available on macos(13)/ios(16), H.264 hardware encoder on Apple Silicon supports it, but HEVC hardware encoder may return kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr depending on OS version.
verdict.evidence:
Reading the actual SDK headers on the local machine (macOS 15.4 SDK and iOS SDK from Xcode) and the FFmpeg source confirms every substantive claim, with two minor corrections:

**1. Symbol availability — CONFIRMED.**
`VTCompressionProperties.h` (macOS 15.4 SDK, line 344; iOS SDK, line 345) declares:
```c
VT_EXPORT const CFStringRef kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ConstantBitRate
  API_AVAILABLE(macos(13.0), ios(16.0), tvos(16.0), visionos(1.0))
  API_UNAVAILABLE(watchos); // CFNumber bits per second, Optional
```
The key exists and its availability annotation is exactly macos(13)/ios(16) — confirmed.

**2. "Not supported in all encoders" warning — CONFIRMED, line numbers slightly off.**
The macOS 15.4 SDK header (macOS SDK lines 340–341, iOS SDK lines 340–341) states verbatim:
> "This is not supported in all encoders or in all encoder operating modes. kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr will be returned when this option is not supported."
The claim cited lines 343–344; the actual lines are 340–341 in the macOS 15.4 SDK. This is a minor numbering discrepancy (SDK versions differ in line count); the text is confirmed word-for-word.

**3. Apple Developer Forums thread 729545 — CONFIRMED, with a nuance.**
The thread exists and a second poster ("yel") did receive `kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr` when attempting `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ConstantBitRate`. However, the forum post does NOT specify which codec (H.264 or HEVC) "yel" was using — the claim that this was specifically HEVC is an inference, not established by the thread. The thread discusses both H.264 and HEVC, and "yel" did not state the codec. The general experience (property not supported) is confirmed; the attribution to HEVC specifically is unverified.

**4. FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c error handling — CONFIRMED, exact lines match.**
FFmpeg HEAD (commit 59619a1), `libavcodec/videotoolboxenc.c` lines 1269–1276:
```c
if (constant_bit_rate) {
    status = VTSessionSetProperty(vtctx->session,
                                  compat_keys.kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ConstantBitRate,
                                  bit_rate_num);
    if (status == kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr) {
        av_log(avctx, AV_LOG_ERROR, "-constant_bit_rate true is not supported by the encoder.\n");
        return AVERROR_EXTERNAL;
    }
}
```
This is codec-agnostic (applies to any non-ProRes codec including both H.264 and HEVC). The claim says "1273–1276" — these are the exact lines in the current source. Confirmed.

**5. EnableLowLatencyRateControl incompatibility with ConstantBitRate — PARTIALLY CONFIRMED with a correction.**
The claim says "kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is explicitly incompatible with ConstantBitRate (header comment line 335)." Reading both SDK headers reveals that the `EnableLowLatencyRateControl` section itself does NOT mention `ConstantBitRate` incompatibility in its own doc comment (neither in macOS 15.4 SDK lines 1119–1134 nor in the iOS SDK lines 1187–1202). The incompatibility IS documented, but it appears in the `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_VariableBitRate` section (iOS SDK lines 367/381, macOS(26) new key) which lists ConstantBitRate and EnableLowLatencyRateControl as mutually incompatible. Additionally, iOS SDK line 1524 states a different property "is ignored when `VTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl` is set to true." The ConstantBitRate doc comment itself does NOT call out LLR incompatibility directly. However, the relationship is implied by the docs' use-case guidance (line 62: LLR is for ultra-low-latency conferencing; line 75: CBR is for legacy CDN — they serve different modes). The claim that the incompatibility is at "header comment line 335" is off — that line is inside the CBR doc block but refers to ExpectedFrameRate, not LLR. The LLR incompatibility is implied architecturally and documented in the VBR/VBVMax sections, not explicitly in a CBR comment. This is a minor inaccuracy in sourcing.

**6. H.264 supports it, HEVC may not — PLAUSIBLE but not confirmed from source.**
No SDK header or FFmpeg comment explicitly says "H.264 HW encoder supports CBR, HEVC HW encoder may not." FFmpeg's code does not guard by codec before setting ConstantBitRate (lines 1263–1276: the only guard is `codec_id != AV_CODEC_ID_PRORES`). The empirical claim rests solely on the forum post, where the codec used when `kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr` was returned was not specified. This remains a reasonable inference from Apple's "not all encoders" language but is not confirmed by source code.
verdict.correction:
Three minor corrections to the claim:

1. Line numbers: The 'not supported in all encoders' warning in VTCompressionProperties.h is at lines 340–341 in the macOS 15.4 SDK (not 343–344 as cited). Line numbering varies by SDK version; the text is otherwise verbatim.

2. EnableLowLatencyRateControl incompatibility sourcing: The claim attributes this incompatibility to 'header comment line 335' within the ConstantBitRate block. In both SDK headers (macOS 15.4 and current iOS), the ConstantBitRate doc block does NOT explicitly mention EnableLowLatencyRateControl as incompatible. The incompatibility is documented elsewhere — specifically in the newer kVTCompressionPropertyKey_VariableBitRate section (iOS SDK lines 367/381, macOS 26 SDK). The practical conclusion (do not use CBR with LLR) is correct by implication from the use-case architecture guidance, but the specific citation is inaccurate.

3. HEVC-specific failure: The forum thread (729545) does not specify which codec the second poster used when receiving kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr. The attribution to HEVC specifically is an inference, not established by the cited source. FFmpeg's videotoolboxenc.c applies the same code path to both H.264 and HEVC (the only codec excluded from bitrate setting is ProRes). Whether HEVC CBR support differs from H.264 by OS version requires on-device probing with VTSessionCopySupportedPropertyDictionary — the header and source do not distinguish them.

verdict.claim: Does kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowTemporalCompression=false (all-intra) force HEVC to software encode on Apple Silicon?
verdict.evidence:
The claim is assessed as **uncertain** — neither confirmed nor refuted — and the SPIKE-ONLY classification is correct. Key findings from primary sources:

**What the SDK header actually says (macOS 15.4 and macOS 26.5 SDKs, `VTCompressionProperties.h` lines 163-170):**
`kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowTemporalCompression` is documented only as: "True by default. Set this to false to require key-frame-only compression." The header carries zero text about hardware/software selection, fallback, or hardware encoder capability limitations for this property.

**`RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder` docs (same header, lines 791-807):** Lists "the requested encoding format or encoding configuration is not supported" as a reason hardware may be unavailable — but this is a generic enumeration, not a specific statement linking `AllowTemporalCompression=false` to hardware encoder failure.

**FFmpeg `videotoolboxenc.c` (master branch, confirmed by grep):** The string `AllowTemporalCompression` does not appear anywhere in the file (zero matches). All-intra HEVC is achieved via `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameInterval` (line 1388). `kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder` is set at line 1704 and coexists with `MaxKeyFrameInterval=1` without any explicit software-fallback guard in that code path. This confirms the claim's specific FFmpeg assertion.

**Apple Developer Forums thread 87047 (the 2017 HAL HEVC thread):** Content confirmed to be about `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AverageBitRate` and `kVTCompressionPropertyKey_DataRateLimits` not working on the hardware HEVC encoder. `AllowTemporalCompression` is not mentioned. The claim's characterization of this thread is accurate.

**No authoritative source found** — neither Apple's official documentation, SDK headers (macOS 10.8 through macOS 26.5), Apple Developer Forum threads, nor FFmpeg source — states that setting `AllowTemporalCompression=false` forces or triggers software encoding for HEVC on Apple Silicon. Equally, no source affirmatively confirms that it stays hardware. The behavior is simply undocumented.

**The claim's proposed alternative** (use `MaxKeyFrameInterval=1` instead of `AllowTemporalCompression=false` to achieve all-intra without risking hardware-encoder selection issues) is well-supported by FFmpeg's production implementation and is the safer approach regardless of outcome.
verdict.correction: The claim is accurate as stated. No correction is needed. The original resolution correctly classifies this as SPIKE-ONLY. The SDK headers (verified on both macOS 15.4 and macOS 26.5) confirm `AllowTemporalCompression` is Read/Write CFBoolean with no documented hardware/software fallback behavior for this specific property. The `RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder` docs note that unsupported encoding configurations may cause `VTCompressionSessionCreate` to fail, but do not enumerate `AllowTemporalCompression=false` as such a configuration. The only way to determine the actual runtime behavior on Apple Silicon hardware is to create a session with both `AllowTemporalCompression=false` and `RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder=true` and observe whether the session succeeds or returns an error — exactly the spike described. The FFmpeg approach of using `MaxKeyFrameInterval=1` (confirmed present at line 1388 of videotoolboxenc.c) is the documented and battle-tested path to all-intra HEVC hardware encoding.
